steps.text: Record and maintain the certification of training or competency for each covered employee.
Give skilled support personnel an initial briefing at the site.
Ensure specialist employees receive training or demonstrate competency in their area of specialization annually.

preconditions.text: Prior to skilled support personnel's participation in any emergency response.

constraints.text: The initial briefing must include instruction in the wearing of appropriate personal protective equipment.
The initial briefing must include instruction on what chemical hazards are involved.
All other appropriate safety and health precautions provided to the employer’s own employees shall be used to assure the safety and health of these personnel.

steps.text: Train first responders at the operations level for at least eight hours (or verify sufficient experience) to demonstrate competency in basic hazard assessment, PPE selection, basic terms, control/containment, decontamination, and SOPs.
Certify that first responders at the operational level have met the competency requirements.
Train hazardous materials technicians for at least 24 hours equal to the first responder operations level, plus additional technician-level competencies.
Certify that hazardous materials technicians have met the competency requirements.
Review the principles and practice for proper selection and use of personal protective equipment.
Review the principles and practice of personnel and equipment decontamination.
Review the expected hazards including fire and explosions hazards, confined space hazards, electrical hazards, powered equipment hazards, motor vehicle hazards, and walking-working surface hazards.

Develop awareness and knowledge of the competencies for the First Responder at the Operations Level covered in the National Fire Protection Association’s Standard No. 472, Professional Competence of Responders to Hazardous Materials Incidents.
Review and demonstrate competency in performing the applicable skills of 29 CFR 1910.120(q).

Gain hands-on experience with written and electronic information relative to response decision making including but not limited to the U.S. Department of Transportation’s Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG), manufacturer material safety data sheets, CHEMTREC/CANUTEC, shipper or manufacturer contacts, computer data bases and response models, and other relevant sources of information addressing hazardous substance releases, and familiarize with OSHA standard 29 CFR 1910.1201.

Review the principles and practices for analyzing an incident to determine the hazardous substances present, their physical and chemical properties, the likely behavior of the hazardous substance and its container, the types of hazardous substance transportation containers and vehicles involved in the release, the appropriate strategy for approaching release sites and containing the release.

Review procedures for implementing continuing response actions consistent with the local emergency response plan, the organization’s standard operating procedures, and the current edition of DOT’s ERG including extended emergency notification procedures and follow-up communications.
Review the principles and practice for proper selection and use of personal protective equipment.
Train qualified persons in the skills and techniques necessary to distinguish exposed live parts from other parts of electric equipment.
Train qualified persons in the skills and techniques necessary to determine the nominal voltage of exposed live parts.
Train qualified persons in the clearance distances specified in 1910.333(c) and the corresponding voltages to which they will be exposed.
Determine the degree of training provided based on the risk to the employee.
Ensure each dive team member has the experience or training necessary to perform assigned tasks in a safe and healthful manner.
Train each dive team member in the use of tools, equipment, and systems relevant to assigned tasks.

preconditions.text: The person must have the training required by paragraph (b)(3) of this section in order to be considered a qualified person.
Qualified persons' work on energized equipment involves either direct contact or contact by means of tools or materials.
Workers' work or the work of those they supervise brings them close enough to exposed parts of electric circuits operating at 50 volts or more to ground for a hazard to exist.

constraints.text: The training required by this section shall be of the classroom or on-the-job type.

steps.text: Institute a training program and ensure employee participation in the program.

Provide training to employees, informing them of AN details including Appendices A and B, quantity/location/use, respirators, medical surveillance, emergency procedures, engineering controls, and a review of the standard.
Make a copy of this standard and its appendices readily available to all affected employees.
Provide all materials relating to the employee information and training program to the Assistant Secretary and the Director.
Provide employees with information and training on EtO.
Inform employees of EtO details including requirements of this section, operations where EtO is present, location/availability of the written EtO final rule, and the medical surveillance program.

preconditions.text: Upon request by the Assistant Secretary and the Director
Employees are potentially exposed to EtO at or above the action level or above the excursion limit

constraints.text: Training shall be provided at the time of initial assignment, or upon institution of the training program, and at least annually thereafter.
Information and training on EtO must be provided at the time of initial assignment and at least annually thereafter.

steps.text: Inform each employee exposed to BD over the action level or STEL of BD health hazards, quantity, location, manner of use, release, storage, engineering controls, work practices, emergency procedures, PPE, protective measures, standard contents, and rights to medical exams, records, and air monitoring.
Make a copy of this standard and its appendices readily available to all affected employees and their designated representatives.
Provide a copy of the standard and its appendices.
Provide all materials relating to the employee information and training program to the Assistant Secretary, the Director, or designated employee representatives.
Provide information and training for each affected employee regarding Methylene Chloride (MC).

preconditions.text: Employee is exposed to BD over the action level or STEL.
A copy of the standard is requested by affected employees or their designated representatives.
A request is made by the Assistant Secretary, the Director, or designated employee representatives.
Prior to or at the time of initial assignment to a job involving potential exposure to MC.

constraints.text: The standard and its appendices must be made available without cost.
Ensure that information and training is presented in a manner that is understandable to the employees.

steps.text: A competent person shall make frequent tests to ascertain the concentration of solvent vapors.

A competent person shall inspect all power lighting cables to ensure insulation is in excellent condition, free of cracks/worn spots, no connections within 50 feet, lines not overloaded, and suspended with sufficient slack.
Provide ventilation in sufficient quantities to keep the concentration of vapors below 10 percent of their lower explosive limit.
A competent person shall make frequent tests to ascertain the vapor concentration.

A competent person shall inspect all power and lighting cables to ensure insulation is in excellent condition, free of cracks/worn spots, no connections within 50 feet, lines not overloaded, and suspended with sufficient slack.
A competent person shall make a test to determine the flammability of the preservative coating.
A competent person shall test the atmosphere in the enclosed space.

preconditions.text: Before welding, cutting or heating is commenced on any surface covered by a preservative coating whose flammability is not known
Before welding, cutting or heating is commenced in enclosed spaces on metals covered by soft and greasy preservatives

constraints.text: Concentration of solvent vapors must be kept below 10 percent of the lower explosive limit.
Preservative coatings shall be considered to be highly flammable when scrapings burn with extreme rapidity.

steps.text: Train employees in the applicable requirements of this section.
Ensure all employees are trained on emergency alarm signals and primary/secondary evacuation routes.
Ensure employees expected to fight incipient stage fires are trained on fire extinguisher/hose line usage, hazards, and system operation.
Establish a written training policy stating fire response employees must be trained and capable.
Keep and update written standard operating procedures addressing anticipated emergency operations.
Review fire response employee training programs and hands-on sessions before they are used.
Provide training for fire response employees that ensures they are protected.

preconditions.text: Employee is currently working (train within 90 days of December 14, 2004).
Employee is newly assigned (train upon initial assignment).
Employee was previously trained (train when necessary to maintain proficiency).
Employees are expected to fight incipient stage fires.
Employees are shipyard employees designated for fire response.

constraints.text: While all vessels and vessel sections must have a primary evacuation route, a secondary evacuation route is not required when impracticable.
Review training programs to make sure that fire response employees are protected from hazards associated with fire response training.

steps.text: Institute a training program for employees performing Class I through IV asbestos operations.
Ensure employee participation in the training program.
Provide training prior to or at the time of initial assignment.
Provide training at least annually thereafter.

preconditions.text: Employee performs Class I through IV asbestos operations.
Employee performs Class I operations or Class II operations requiring critical barriers and/or negative pressure enclosures.
Employee performs Class II work with asbestos containing roofing, flooring, siding, ceiling tiles, or transite panels.
Employee performs Class II operations not involving the categories of material specified in paragraph (k)(9)(iv)(A).

constraints.text: Training shall be provided at no cost to the employee.
Training must be equivalent in curriculum, training method, and length to the EPA Model Accreditation Plan (MAP) asbestos abatement workers training.
Training must include at a minimum all elements in paragraph (k)(9)(viii), specific work practices/engineering controls in paragraph (g), hands-on training, and take at least 8 hours.
An employee working with more than one category of material must receive training in the work practices applicable to each category of material removed and each removal method used.

steps.text: Train each employee involved in maintaining the on-going integrity of process equipment in an overview of that process, its hazards, and the procedures applicable to the employee's job tasks.
Inform and train employees (operating, maintenance, and contract) whose job tasks will be affected by a change in the process on the change.

preconditions.text: Training of each employee involved in operating a process has been completed prior to the introduction of highly hazardous chemicals to a process.
Prior to start-up of the process or affected part of the process.

constraints.text: Assure that the employee can perform the job tasks in a safe manner.

steps.text: Make available to affected employees any information concerning the chemical, physical, and toxicologic properties of each substance known or expected to be present on site.
Train all employees working on site (and their supervisors/management responsible for the site) meeting the requirements of this paragraph.

preconditions.text: Prior to the commencement of their work activities.
Before employees are permitted to engage in hazardous waste operations that could expose them to hazardous substances, safety, or health hazards.
Employees shall not be permitted to participate in or supervise field activities until they have been trained to a level required by their job function and responsibility.

constraints.text: Training must thoroughly cover: names of responsible personnel, safety/health hazards, use of PPE, work practices to minimize risks, safe use of engineering controls, medical surveillance requirements, and contents of paragraphs (G) through (J) of the site safety and health plan.

steps.text: Certify that the hazardous materials technician has met the training and competency requirements.

preconditions.text: The technician must have received at least 24 hours of training equal to the first responder operations level.

The technician must demonstrate competency in implementing the employer's emergency response plan, classifying/identifying materials, functioning in the Incident Command System, selecting/using PPE, hazard/risk assessment, performing control/containment, implementing decontamination, understanding termination, and understanding chemical/toxicological terminology.

constraints.text: Select and use proper specialized chemical personal protective equipment provided to the hazardous materials technician.

steps.text: Provide skilled support personnel with a safety and health briefing.
Make skilled support personnel aware of the hazards they face.
Provide skilled support personnel with all necessary protective clothing and equipment required for their tasks.
Allow skilled support personnel to enter the area of potential or actual exposure.

preconditions.text: Skilled support personnel have not been a part of the emergency response plan and do not meet the training requirements.

constraints.text: Skilled support personnel must be provided all necessary protective clothing and equipment required for their tasks.
Skilled support personnel must be made aware of the hazards they face.

steps.text: Remove electrodes from holders and place or protect the holders so they cannot make electrical contact with employees or conducting objects.
Open the power supply switch to the equipment.
Report any faulty or defective equipment to the supervisor.
Assign additional personnel to guard against fire while the actual welding, cutting, or heating operation is being performed.
Instruct the assigned fire guard personnel as to the specific anticipated fire hazards and how the firefighting equipment provided is to be used.
Take the same precautions on the opposite side of the wall, floor, or ceiling as are taken on the side on which the welding is being performed.
Ascertain by inquiry [the hazards present].

preconditions.text: Electrode holders are to be left unattended.
The arc welder or cutter has occasion to leave work, stop work for any appreciable length of time, or the arc welding or cutting machine is to be moved.
The welding, cutting, or heating operation is such that normal fire prevention precautions are not sufficient.
Welding, cutting, or heating is performed on walls, floors, and ceilings where direct penetration of sparks or heat transfer may introduce a fire hazard to an adjacent area.
Before work is begun.

constraints.text: Hot electrode holders shall not be dipped in water; to do so may expose the arc welder or cutter to electric shock.
Additional fire guard personnel must remain for a sufficient period of time after completion of the work to ensure that no possibility of fire exists.

steps.text: Provide a training program for each employee who might be exposed to fall hazards.
Assure that each employee has been trained, as necessary, by a competent person qualified in the required fall hazard and protection areas.
Verify compliance with training requirements by preparing a written certification record.
Maintain the latest training certification.

preconditions.text: If the employer relies on training conducted by another employer or completed prior to the effective date of this section, the certification record must indicate the date the employer determined the prior training was adequate.

constraints.text: The training program shall enable each employee to recognize the hazards of falling and train them in procedures to minimize these hazards.
The written certification record must contain the name or identity of the employee, the training date(s), and the signature of the trainer or employer.

steps.text: A competent person shall evaluate the work area, the projected work practices, and the engineering controls.
Certify in writing that the different or modified controls are adequate to reduce direct and indirect employee exposure to below the PELs and that the method meets the requirements of this standard.
Conduct an inspection of the worksite.
Determine that the roofing material is intact and will likely remain intact.

preconditions.text: Before work begins and as needed during the job

constraints.text: All employees performing work covered by this paragraph (g)(11) shall be trained in a training program that meets the requirements of paragraph (k)(9)(viii) of this section.

steps.text: Conduct training for Class III employees consistent with EPA requirements, including hands-on training.
Conduct training for employees performing Class IV operations consistent with EPA requirements, including locations of ACM/PACM and recognition of damage.
Conduct training for employees likely to be exposed in excess of the PEL who are not otherwise trained under Class I through IV.
Conduct the training program in an understandable manner and ensure each employee is informed of the required content.

preconditions.text: The competent person determines that the EPA curriculum does not adequately cover the training needed for a Class III activity.
Employees are likely to be exposed in excess of the PEL and are not otherwise required to be trained under paragraph (k)(9)(iii) through (vi).

constraints.text: Class III training course must take at least 16 hours.
Class IV training course must take at least 2 hours.

steps.text: Contact each other prior to the operation and agree on the non-standard hand signals that will be used.
Train each employee who may be exposed to fall hazards while on, or hoisted by, equipment on the fall protection requirements in this subpart and in 1926.500 and 1926.502.
Train each employee assigned to work on or near the equipment ('authorized personnel') in how to recognize struck-by and pinch/crush hazard areas posed by the rotating superstructure.
Erect and maintain control lines, warning lines, railings or similar barriers to mark the boundaries of the hazard areas.

Clearly mark the hazard areas by a combination of warning signs (such as 'Danger—Swing/Crush Zone') and high visibility markings on the equipment, and train each employee to understand what these markings signify.

preconditions.text: When using non-standard hand signals
When the employer can demonstrate that it is neither feasible to erect barriers on the ground nor on the equipment

constraints.text: Employees working as dedicated spotters must be trained to enable them to effectively perform their task, including training on the applicable requirements of this section.
Training under this section must be administered in accordance with 1926.1430(g).
Swing radius hazards: Prevent employees from entering these hazard areas.
